questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A laboratory needs to construct a precision standard resistor of exactly 100 Ω at 20 ∘ C . The engineers are comparing two materials: Copper ( α C u ≈ 4 × 10 − 3 ∘ C − 1 ) and Manganin ( α M n ≈ 2 × 10 − 6 ∘ C − 1 ). If the ambient temperature of the laboratory fluctuates by 10 ∘ C due to a faulty HVAC system, what is the approximate ratio of the absolute change in resistance of the Copper resistor to that of the Manganin resistor?

questions.html: A laboratory needs to construct a precision standard resistor of exactly 100 Ω at 20∘C. The engineers are comparing two materials: Copper (αCu≈4×10−3 ∘C−1) and Manganin (αMn≈2×10−6 ∘C−1). If the ambient temperature of the laboratory fluctuates by 10∘C due to a faulty HVAC system, what is the approximate ratio of the absolute change in resistance of the Copper resistor to that of the Manganin resistor?
questions.questionHtml: A laboratory needs to construct a precision standard resistor of exactly 100 Ω at 20∘C. The engineers are comparing two materials: Copper (αCu≈4×10−3 ∘C−1) and Manganin (αMn≈2×10−6 ∘C−1). If the ambient temperature of the laboratory fluctuates by 10∘C due to a faulty HVAC system, what is the approximate ratio of the absolute change in resistance of the Copper resistor to that of the Manganin resistor?
questions.solutionText: Solution Change in resistance: Δ R = R 0 ⋅ α ⋅ Δ T Since R 0 = 100 Ω and Δ T = 10 ∘ C are the same for both: Δ R C u = 100 × 4 × 10 − 3 × 10 = 4 Ω Δ R M n = 100 × 2 × 10 − 6 × 10 = 2 × 10 − 3 Ω Δ R C u Δ R M n = 4 2 × 10 − 3 = 2000 The copper resistor changes 2000 times more than the manganin resistor, which is why manganin is preferred for precision standard resistors.
questions.solution: Solution Change in resistance: Δ R = R 0 ⋅ α ⋅ Δ T Since R 0 = 100 Ω and Δ T = 10 ∘ C are the same for both: Δ R C u = 100 × 4 × 10 − 3 × 10 = 4 Ω Δ R M n = 100 × 2 × 10 − 6 × 10 = 2 × 10 − 3 Ω Δ R C u Δ R M n = 4 2 × 10 − 3 = 2000 The copper resistor changes 2000 times more than the manganin resistor, which is why manganin is preferred for precision standard resistors.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Change in resistance: ΔR=R0⋅α⋅ΔT

Since R0=100 Ω and ΔT=10∘C are the same for both:

ΔRCu=100×4×10−3×10=4 Ω

ΔRMn=100×2×10−6×10=2×10−3 Ω

ΔRCuΔRMn=42×10−3=2000

The copper resistor changes 2000 times more than the manganin resistor, which is why manganin is preferred for precision standard resistors.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two coherent light sources, S 1 and S 2 , emit waves of wavelength λ . Source S 2 has an initial phase lead of π 3 radians relative to S 1 . The intensity of S 1 is I 0 and the intensity of S 2 is 9 I 0 . The waves interfere at a point P , where the path length from S 1 to P is exactly λ 12 longer than the path from S 2 to P . What is the resultant intensity at point P , expressed as a fraction of the maximum possible intensity ( I m a x ) of this interference pattern?
questions.questionText: Two coherent light sources, S 1 and S 2 , emit waves of wavelength λ . Source S 2 has an initial phase lead of π 3 radians relative to S 1 . questions.prompt: Two coherent light sources, S 1 and S 2 , emit waves of wavelength λ . Source S 2 has an initial phase lead of π 3 radians relative to S 1 . The intensity of S 1 is I 0 and the intensity of S 2 is 9 I 0 . The waves interfere at a point P , where the path length from S 1 to P is exactly λ 12 longer than the path from S 2 to P . questions.html: Two coherent light sources, S1 and S2, emit waves of wavelength λ. Source S2 has an initial phase lead of π3 radians relative to S1. The intensity of S1 is I0 and the intensity of S2 is 9I0. The waves interfere at a point P, where the path length from S1 to P is exactly λ12 longer than the path from S2 to P. What is the resultant intensity at point P, expressed as a fraction of the maximum possible intensity (Imax) of this interference pattern?
questions.questionHtml: Two coherent light sources, S1 and S2, emit waves of wavelength λ. Source S2 has an initial phase lead of π3 radians relative to S1. The intensity of S1 is I0 and the intensity of S2 is 9I0. The waves interfere at a point P, where the path length from S1 to P is exactly λ12 longer than the path from S2 to P. What is the resultant intensity at point P, expressed as a fraction of the maximum possible intensity (Imax) of this interference pattern?
questions.solutionText: Solution I 1 = I 0 , I 2 = 9 I 0 ⇒ A 1 ∝ 1 , A 2 ∝ 3 Phase difference due to path difference: Path from S 1 to P is λ 12 longer than from S 2 to P . ϕ 1 = 2 π λ ⋅ λ 12 = π 6 S 2 has an initial phase lead of π 3 relative to S 1 . Total phase difference: ϕ = ϕ 1 + π 3 = π 6 + π 3 = π 6 + 2 π 6 = π 2 Resultant intensity: I = I 1 + I 2 + 2 I 1 I 2 cos ⁡ ϕ I = I 0 + 9 I 0 + 2 9 I 0 2 cos ⁡ π 2 I = 10 I 0 + 6 I 0 ⋅ 0 = 10 I 0 Maximum possible intensity: I m a x = ( I 1 + I 2 ) 2 = ( I 0 + 3 I 0 ) 2 = 16 I 0 I I m a x = 10 I 0 16 I 0 = 5 8
questions.solution: Solution I 1 = I 0 , I 2 = 9 I 0 ⇒ A 1 ∝ 1 , A 2 ∝ 3 Phase difference due to path difference: Path from S 1 to P is λ 12 longer than from S 2 to P . ϕ 1 = 2 π λ ⋅ λ 12 = π 6 S 2 has an initial phase lead of π 3 relative to S 1 . Total phase difference: ϕ = ϕ 1 + π 3 = π 6 + π 3 = π 6 + 2 π 6 = π 2 Resultant intensity: I = I 1 + I 2 + 2 I 1 I 2 cos ⁡ ϕ I = I 0 + 9 I 0 + 2 9 I 0 2 cos ⁡ π 2 I = 10 I 0 + 6 I 0 ⋅ 0 = 10 I 0 Maximum possible intensity: I m a x = ( I 1 + I 2 ) 2 = ( I 0 + 3 I 0 ) 2 = 16 I 0 I I m a x = 10 I 0 16 I 0 = 5 8
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
I1=I0, I2=9I0 ⇒ A1∝1, A2∝3

Phase difference due to path difference:
Path from S1 to P is λ12 longer than from S2 to P.
ϕ1=2πλ⋅λ12=π6

S2 has an initial phase lead of π3 relative to S1.

Total phase difference:
ϕ=ϕ1+π3=π6+π3=π6+2π6=π2

Resultant intensity:
I=I1+I2+2I1I2cos⁡ϕ
I=I0+9I0+29I02cos⁡π2
I=10I0+6I0⋅0=10I0

Maximum possible intensity:
Imax=(I1+I2)2=(I0+3I0)2=16I0

IImax=10I016I0=58

questions.text: Two planets revolve round the sun with frequencies N 1 and N 2 revolutions per year. If their average orbital radii be R 1 and R 2 respectively, then R 1 / R 2 is equal to

questions.prompt: Two planets revolve round the sun with frequencies N 1 and N 2 revolutions per year. If their average orbital radii be R 1 and R 2 respectively, then R 1 / R 2 is equal to
questions.html: Two planets revolve round the sun with frequencies N1 and N2 revolutions per year. If their average orbital radii be R1 and R2 respectively, then R1/R2 is equal to
questions.questionHtml: Two planets revolve round the sun with frequencies N1 and N2 revolutions per year. If their average orbital radii be R1 and R2 respectively, then R1/R2 is equal to
questions.solutionText: Solution By Kepler's third law: T 2 ∝ R 3 Since frequency N = 1 T , we have T = 1 N 1 N 2 ∝ R 3 ⇒ R ∝ 1 N 2 / 3 R 1 R 2 = ( N 2 N 1 ) 2 / 3
questions.solution: Solution By Kepler's third law: T 2 ∝ R 3 Since frequency N = 1 T , we have T = 1 N 1 N 2 ∝ R 3 ⇒ R ∝ 1 N 2 / 3 R 1 R 2 = ( N 2 N 1 ) 2 / 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
By Kepler's third law: T2∝R3

Since frequency N=1T, we have T=1N

1N2∝R3⇒R∝1N2/3

R1R2=(N2N1)2/3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The ends of a uniform metal rod of length 100 c m and area of cross-section 2 c m 2 are maintained at 0 ∘ C and 100 ∘ C . At the mid point of the rod, heat is supplied at a constant rate of 40 J s − 1 . If the temperature gradient in the half of the rod between the midpoint and the 100 ∘ C end in steady state is 50 x ∘ C m − 1 , then the value of x is (Thermal conductivity of the metal = 400 J s − 1 m − 1 K − 1 )

questions.questionHtml: The ends of a uniform metal rod of length 100cm and area of cross-section 2cm2 are maintained at 0∘C and 100∘C. At the mid point of the rod, heat is supplied at a constant rate of 40Js−1. If the temperature gradient in the half of the rod between the midpoint and the 100∘C end in steady state is 50x∘Cm−1, then the value of x is (Thermal conductivity of the metal =400Js−1m−1K−1)
questions.solutionText: Solution L = 100 c m = 1 m , A = 2 c m 2 = 2 × 10 − 4 m 2 , K = 400 J s − 1 m − 1 K − 1 Left end: 0 ∘ C , Right end: 100 ∘ C . Heat supplied at midpoint: 40 J / s Let temperature at midpoint = T (in steady state, T > 100 ∘ C since heat is being added). Heat flows from midpoint to both ends: Q L = K A ⋅ T L / 2 , Q R = K A ( T − 100 ) L / 2 Q L + Q R = 40 K A 0.5 ( T + T − 100 ) = 40 400 × 2 × 10 − 4 0.5 ( 2 T − 100 ) = 40 0.16 ( 2 T − 100 ) = 40 ⇒ 2 T − 100 = 250 ⇒ T = 175 ∘ C Temperature gradient between midpoint and 100 ∘ C end: Δ T Δ x = 175 − 100 0.5 = 150 ∘ C m − 1 50 x = 150 ⇒ x = 3
questions.solution: Solution L = 100 c m = 1 m , A = 2 c m 2 = 2 × 10 − 4 m 2 , K = 400 J s − 1 m − 1 K − 1 Left end: 0 ∘ C , Right end: 100 ∘ C . Heat supplied at midpoint: 40 J / s Let temperature at midpoint = T (in steady state, T > 100 ∘ C since heat is being added). Heat flows from midpoint to both ends: Q L = K A ⋅ T L / 2 , Q R = K A ( T − 100 ) L / 2 Q L + Q R = 40 K A 0.5 ( T + T − 100 ) = 40 400 × 2 × 10 − 4 0.5 ( 2 T − 100 ) = 40 0.16 ( 2 T − 100 ) = 40 ⇒ 2 T − 100 = 250 ⇒ T = 175 ∘ C Temperature gradient between midpoint and 100 ∘ C end: Δ T Δ x = 175 − 100 0.5 = 150 ∘ C m − 1 50 x = 150 ⇒ x = 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
L=100cm=1m, A=2cm2=2×10−4m2, K=400Js−1m−1K−1

Left end: 0∘C, Right end: 100∘C. Heat supplied at midpoint: 40J/s

Let temperature at midpoint =T (in steady state, T>100∘C since heat is being added).

Heat flows from midpoint to both ends:

QL=KA⋅TL/2, QR=KA(T−100)L/2

QL+QR=40

KA0.5(T+T−100)=40

400×2×10−40.5(2T−100)=40

0.16(2T−100)=40⇒2T−100=250⇒T=175∘C

Temperature gradient between midpoint and 100∘C end:

ΔTΔx=175−1000.5=150∘Cm−1

50x=150⇒x=3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An aeroplane of mass M is in level flight. The total area of its wings is 20 m 2 . The speed of the air flowing over the upper surface of the wings is 120 m/s , and the speed of the air flowing under the lower surface is 90 m/s . Assuming the thickness of the wing is negligible (so the height difference between the top and bottom airflows can be ignored), what is the approximate mass of the aeroplane? (Given: Density of air ρ = 1.2 kg/m 3 and acceleration due to gravity g = 10 m/s 2 )

questions.questionHtml: An aeroplane of mass M is in level flight. The total area of its wings is 20 m2. The speed of the air flowing over the upper surface of the wings is 120 m/s, and the speed of the air flowing under the lower surface is 90 m/s. Assuming the thickness of the wing is negligible (so the height difference between the top and bottom airflows can be ignored), what is the approximate mass of the aeroplane? (Given: Density of air ρ=1.2 kg/m3 and acceleration due to gravity g=10 m/s2)
questions.solutionText: Solution In level flight, the lift force equals the weight of the aeroplane. Using Bernoulli's theorem, the pressure difference between lower and upper surfaces: Δ P = 1 2 ρ ( v top 2 − v bottom 2 ) Δ P = 1 2 × 1.2 × ( 120 2 − 90 2 ) = 1 2 × 1.2 × ( 14400 − 8100 ) = 0.6 × 6300 = 3780 P a Lift = Δ P × A = 3780 × 20 = 75600 N For level flight: M g = 75600 M = 75600 10 = 7560 k g
questions.solution: Solution In level flight, the lift force equals the weight of the aeroplane. Using Bernoulli's theorem, the pressure difference between lower and upper surfaces: Δ P = 1 2 ρ ( v top 2 − v bottom 2 ) Δ P = 1 2 × 1.2 × ( 120 2 − 90 2 ) = 1 2 × 1.2 × ( 14400 − 8100 ) = 0.6 × 6300 = 3780 P a Lift = Δ P × A = 3780 × 20 = 75600 N For level flight: M g = 75600 M = 75600 10 = 7560 k g
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
In level flight, the lift force equals the weight of the aeroplane.

Using Bernoulli's theorem, the pressure difference between lower and upper surfaces:

ΔP=12ρ(vtop2−vbottom2)

ΔP=12×1.2×(1202−902)

=12×1.2×(14400−8100)

=0.6×6300=3780Pa

Lift =ΔP×A=3780×20=75600N

For level flight: Mg=75600

M=7560010=7560kg

questions.questionHtml: A material has Poisson's ratio 0.50. If a uniform rod of it suffers a longitudinal strain of 2×10-3, then the percentage change in volume is
questions.solutionText: Solution Poisson's ratio σ = 0.50 Longitudinal strain: Δ L L = 2 × 10 − 3 Lateral strain: Δ r r = − σ ⋅ Δ L L = − 0.5 × 2 × 10 − 3 = − 1 × 10 − 3 For a cylindrical rod, V = π r 2 L : Δ V V = Δ L L + 2 Δ r r = 2 × 10 − 3 + 2 ( − 1 × 10 − 3 ) = 0 Percentage change in volume = 0 % Note: σ = 0.5 corresponds to an incompressible material where volume is conserved under deformation.
questions.solution: Solution Poisson's ratio σ = 0.50 Longitudinal strain: Δ L L = 2 × 10 − 3 Lateral strain: Δ r r = − σ ⋅ Δ L L = − 0.5 × 2 × 10 − 3 = − 1 × 10 − 3 For a cylindrical rod, V = π r 2 L : Δ V V = Δ L L + 2 Δ r r = 2 × 10 − 3 + 2 ( − 1 × 10 − 3 ) = 0 Percentage change in volume = 0 % Note: σ = 0.5 corresponds to an incompressible material where volume is conserved under deformation.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Poisson's ratio σ=0.50

Longitudinal strain: ΔLL=2×10−3

Lateral strain: Δrr=−σ⋅ΔLL=−0.5×2×10−3=−1×10−3

For a cylindrical rod, V=πr2L:

ΔVV=ΔLL+2Δrr=2×10−3+2(−1×10−3)=0

Percentage change in volume =0%

Note: σ=0.5 corresponds to an incompressible material where volume is conserved under deformation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a reflecting telescope, a secondary mirror is used to:
questions.questionText: In a reflecting telescope, a secondary mirror is used to:
questions.question: In a reflecting telescope, a secondary mirror is used to:
questions.prompt: In a reflecting telescope, a secondary mirror is used to:
questions.html: In a reflecting telescope, a secondary mirror is used to:
questions.questionHtml: In a reflecting telescope, a secondary mirror is used to:
questions.solutionText: Solution In a reflecting telescope (e.g., Cassegrain type), the secondary mirror redirects the converging light from the primary mirror back through a hole in the primary mirror, so that the eyepiece can be placed outside the telescopic tube for convenient viewing.
questions.solution: Solution In a reflecting telescope (e.g., Cassegrain type), the secondary mirror redirects the converging light from the primary mirror back through a hole in the primary mirror, so that the eyepiece can be placed outside the telescopic tube for convenient viewing.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
In a reflecting telescope (e.g., Cassegrain type), the secondary mirror redirects the converging light from the primary mirror back through a hole in the primary mirror, so that the eyepiece can be placed outside the telescopic tube for convenient viewing.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An inductor of inductance L , a capacitor of capacitance C , and a resistor of resistance R are connected in series to an AC source. The circuit has a resonant angular frequency ω 0 and a quality factor Q . To improve the selectivity of the circuit, you want to double the sharpness of the resonance. However, you must keep the resonant frequency ω 0 exactly the same. Assuming the resistance R remains constant, what should be the new values of inductance ( L ′ ) and capacitance ( C ′ )?

The circuit has a resonant angular frequency ω 0 and a quality factor Q . To improve the selectivity of the circuit, you want to double the sharpness of the resonance. However, you must keep the resonant frequency ω 0 exactly the same. Assuming the resistance R remains constant, what should be the new values of inductance ( L ′ ) and capacitance ( C ′ )?
questions.html: An inductor of inductance L, a capacitor of capacitance C, and a resistor of resistance R are connected in series to an AC source. The circuit has a resonant angular frequency ω0 and a quality factor Q. To improve the selectivity of the circuit, you want to double the sharpness of the resonance. However, you must keep the resonant frequency ω0 exactly the same. Assuming the resistance R remains constant, what should be the new values of inductance (L′) and capacitance (C′)?
questions.questionHtml: An inductor of inductance L, a capacitor of capacitance C, and a resistor of resistance R are connected in series to an AC source. The circuit has a resonant angular frequency ω0 and a quality factor Q. To improve the selectivity of the circuit, you want to double the sharpness of the resonance. However, you must keep the resonant frequency ω0 exactly the same. Assuming the resistance R remains constant, what should be the new values of inductance (L′) and capacitance (C′)?
questions.solutionText: Solution We need Q ′ = 2 Q while keeping ω 0 ′ = ω 0 and R constant. Key formulas: Q = 1 R L C and ω 0 = 1 L C Condition 1: ω 0 ′ = ω 0 1 L ′ C ′ = 1 L C ⇒ L ′ C ′ = L C Condition 2: Q ′ = 2 Q 1 R L ′ C ′ = 2 R L C ⇒ L ′ C ′ = 4 ⋅ L C From these two equations: L ′ C ′ = L C . . . ( i ) L ′ C ′ = 4 L C . . . ( i i ) Multiply (i) and (ii): L ′ 2 = 4 L 2 ⇒ L ′ = 2 L Substitute back in (i): 2 L ⋅ C ′ = L C ⇒ C ′ = C 2
questions.solution: Solution We need Q ′ = 2 Q while keeping ω 0 ′ = ω 0 and R constant. Key formulas: Q = 1 R L C and ω 0 = 1 L C Condition 1: ω 0 ′ = ω 0 1 L ′ C ′ = 1 L C ⇒ L ′ C ′ = L C Condition 2: Q ′ = 2 Q 1 R L ′ C ′ = 2 R L C ⇒ L ′ C ′ = 4 ⋅ L C From these two equations: L ′ C ′ = L C . . . ( i ) L ′ C ′ = 4 L C . . . ( i i ) Multiply (i) and (ii): L ′ 2 = 4 L 2 ⇒ L ′ = 2 L Substitute back in (i): 2 L ⋅ C ′ = L C ⇒ C ′ = C 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
We need Q′=2Q while keeping ω0′=ω0 and R constant.

Key formulas:
Q=1RLC and ω0=1LC

Condition 1: ω0′=ω0
1L′C′=1LC⇒L′C′=LC

Condition 2: Q′=2Q
1RL′C′=2RLC⇒L′C′=4⋅LC

From these two equations:
L′C′=LC...(i)
L′C′=4LC...(ii)

Multiply (i) and (ii):
L′2=4L2⇒L′=2L

Substitute back in (i):
2L⋅C′=LC⇒C′=C2

The coefficient of kinetic friction between the lawnmower and the grass is μ k = 0.5 .Assuming the force is always applied exactly along the line of the handle, what is the ratio of the magnitude of the force required to push the lawnmower to the force required to pull it ( F p u s h F p u l l )? questions.questionHtml:
A gardener needs to move a lawnmower of mass m at a constant velocity across a horizontal lawn. The handle of the lawnmower makes an angle of 37∘ with the horizontal. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the lawnmower and the grass is μk=0.5.Assuming the force is always applied exactly along the line of the handle, what is the ratio of the magnitude of the force required to push the lawnmower to the force required to pull it (FpushFpull)?
(Take sin⁡37∘=35 and cos⁡37∘=45)
questions.solutionText: Solution For constant velocity, net force = 0. Case 1: Pushing (force F p at 37 ∘ below horizontal) Vertical: N = m g + F p sin ⁡ 37 ∘ Horizontal: F p cos ⁡ 37 ∘ = μ k N = 0.5 ( m g + F p sin ⁡ 37 ∘ ) F p ⋅ 4 5 = 0.5 ( m g + F p ⋅ 3 5 ) 4 F p 5 = m g 2 + 3 F p 10 4 F p 5 − 3 F p 10 = m g 2 5 F p 10 = m g 2 F p = m g Case 2: Pulling (force F l at 37 ∘ above horizontal) Vertical: N = m g − F l sin ⁡ 37 ∘ Horizontal: F l cos ⁡ 37 ∘ = 0.5 ( m g − F l sin ⁡ 37 ∘ ) F l ⋅ 4 5 = 0.5 ( m g − F l ⋅ 3 5 ) 4 F l 5 = m g 2 − 3 F l 10 4 F l 5 + 3 F l 10 = m g 2 11 F l 10 = m g 2 F l = 5 m g 11 F p u s h F p u l l = m g 5 m g 11 = 11 5
questions.solution: Solution For constant velocity, net force = 0. Case 1: Pushing (force F p at 37 ∘ below horizontal) Vertical: N = m g + F p sin ⁡ 37 ∘ Horizontal: F p cos ⁡ 37 ∘ = μ k N = 0.5 ( m g + F p sin ⁡ 37 ∘ ) F p ⋅ 4 5 = 0.5 ( m g + F p ⋅ 3 5 ) 4 F p 5 = m g 2 + 3 F p 10 4 F p 5 − 3 F p 10 = m g 2 5 F p 10 = m g 2 F p = m g Case 2: Pulling (force F l at 37 ∘ above horizontal) Vertical: N = m g − F l sin ⁡ 37 ∘ Horizontal: F l cos ⁡ 37 ∘ = 0.5 ( m g − F l sin ⁡ 37 ∘ ) F l ⋅ 4 5 = 0.5 ( m g − F l ⋅ 3 5 ) 4 F l 5 = m g 2 − 3 F l 10 4 F l 5 + 3 F l 10 = m g 2 11 F l 10 = m g 2 F l = 5 m g 11 F p u s h F p u l l = m g 5 m g 11 = 11 5
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For constant velocity, net force = 0.

Case 1: Pushing (force Fp at 37∘ below horizontal)
Vertical: N=mg+Fpsin⁡37∘
Horizontal: Fpcos⁡37∘=μkN=0.5(mg+Fpsin⁡37∘)

Fp⋅45=0.5(mg+Fp⋅35)
4Fp5=mg2+3Fp10
4Fp5−3Fp10=mg2
5Fp10=mg2
Fp=mg

Case 2: Pulling (force Fl at 37∘ above horizontal)
Vertical: N=mg−Flsin⁡37∘
Horizontal: Flcos⁡37∘=0.5(mg−Flsin⁡37∘)

Fl⋅45=0.5(mg−Fl⋅35)
4Fl5=mg2−3Fl10
4Fl5+3Fl10=mg2
11Fl10=mg2
Fl=5mg11

FpushFpull=mg5mg11=115

Two equal forces of magnitude A with resultant 2A3.

Using the resultant formula:

4A29=2A2+2A2cos⁡θ

questions.solutionText: Solution The speed of transverse pulse on a string is given by v = T μ Where, T = Tension in the string. μ = Mass per unit length of the wire. Given, T = 100 N , m = 6 × 10 - 3 kg , L = 90 cm = 0 . 9 m So, v = T μ = T M L ⇒ v = 100 × 0 . 9 6 × 10 - 3 = 50 6 m s - 1 `

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

The speed of transverse pulse on a string is given by

v=Tμ

Where, T= Tension in the string.

μ= Mass per unit length of the wire.

Given, T=100 N, m=6×10-3 kg, L=90 cm=0.9 m

So, v=Tμ=TML

⇒ v=100×0.96×10-3=506 m s-1


`

questions.solutionText: Solution u = g 2 (downward), a = g (downward) Work done by gravity in n t h second = m g × (distance in n t h second) Distance in n t h second: S n = u + g 2 ( 2 n − 1 ) S 1 = g 2 + g 2 ( 1 ) = g S 2 = g 2 + g 2 ( 3 ) = 2 g S 3 = g 2 + g 2 ( 5 ) = 3 g W 1 : W 2 : W 3 = S 1 : S 2 : S 3 = g : 2 g : 3 g W 1 : W 2 : W 3 = 1 : 2 : 3 ^
questions.solution: Solution u = g 2 (downward), a = g (downward) Work done by gravity in n t h second = m g × (distance in n t h second) Distance in n t h second: S n = u + g 2 ( 2 n − 1 ) S 1 = g 2 + g 2 ( 1 ) = g S 2 = g 2 + g 2 ( 3 ) = 2 g S 3 = g 2 + g 2 ( 5 ) = 3 g W 1 : W 2 : W 3 = S 1 : S 2 : S 3 = g : 2 g : 3 g W 1 : W 2 : W 3 = 1 : 2 : 3 ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
u=g2 (downward), a=g (downward)

Work done by gravity in nth second = mg× (distance in nth second)

Distance in nth second: Sn=u+g2(2n−1)

S1=g2+g2(1)=g

S2=g2+g2(3)=2g

S3=g2+g2(5)=3g

W1:W2:W3=S1:S2:S3=g:2g:3g

W1:W2:W3=1:2:3
^

questions.solutionText: Solution Let us take x mg iodine migrated to thyroid gland. if A is activity of complete sample of mass m . Then activity of x is given by A x = A m x A x is 67.7 % of initial total sample at t = 4 days. A x = 67.7 100 A 0 & A = A 0 2 t / T T = half life time So using above equation, 67.7 100 A 0 = A 0 2 4 / 8 x m x m × 100 = 67.7 × 2 = 95.7 % *
questions.solution: Solution Let us take x mg iodine migrated to thyroid gland. if A is activity of complete sample of mass m . questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let us take x mg iodine migrated to thyroid gland.
if A is activity of complete sample of mass m. Then activity of x is given by
Ax=Amx
Ax is 67.7% of initial total sample at t=4 days.
Ax=67.7100A0 & A=A02t/T
T=half life time
So using above equation,
67.7100A0=A024/8 xm
xm×100=67.7×2=95.7%
*

questions.solutionText: Solution Work done by gravity = change in kinetic energy = K E a − K E p Using conservation of angular momentum at perihelion and aphelion: m v p R = m v a ( 3 R ) ⇒ v a = v p 3 Using conservation of energy: 1 2 m v p 2 − G M m R = 1 2 m v a 2 − G M m 3 R 1 2 v p 2 − 1 2 ⋅ v p 2 9 = G M R − G M 3 R v p 2 2 ( 1 − 1 9 ) = G M ( 1 R − 1 3 R ) v p 2 2 ⋅ 8 9 = 2 G M 3 R v p 2 = 3 G M 2 R v a 2 = v p 2 9 = G M 6 R Work done by gravity (perihelion to aphelion): W = K E a − K E p = 1 2 m v a 2 − 1 2 m v p 2 = 1 2 m ( G M 6 R − 3 G M 2 R ) = 1 2 m ⋅ G M ( 1 6 R − 9 6 R ) = G M m 2 ⋅ − 8 6 R = − 2 G M m 3 R Magnitude = 2 G M m 3 R !
questions.solution: Solution Work done by gravity = change in kinetic energy = K E a − K E p Using conservation of angular momentum at perihelion and aphelion: m v p R = m v a ( 3 R ) ⇒ v a = v p 3 Using conservation of energy: 1 2 m v p 2 − G M m R = 1 2 m v a 2 − G M m 3 R 1 2 v p 2 − 1 2 ⋅ v p 2 9 = G M R − G M 3 R v p 2 2 ( 1 − 1 9 ) = G M ( 1 R − 1 3 R ) v p 2 2 ⋅ 8 9 = 2 G M 3 R v p 2 = 3 G M 2 R v a 2 = v p 2 9 = G M 6 R Work done by gravity (perihelion to aphelion): W = K E a − K E p = 1 2 m v a 2 − 1 2 m v p 2 = 1 2 m ( G M 6 R − 3 G M 2 R ) = 1 2 m ⋅ G M ( 1 6 R − 9 6 R ) = G M m 2 ⋅ − 8 6 R = − 2 G M m 3 R Magnitude = 2 G M m 3 R !
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Work done by gravity = change in kinetic energy =KEa−KEp

Using conservation of angular momentum at perihelion and aphelion:

mvpR=mva(3R)⇒va=vp3

Using conservation of energy:

12mvp2−GMmR=12mva2−GMm3R

12vp2−12⋅vp29=GMR−GM3R

vp22(1−19)=GM(1R−13R)

vp22⋅89=2GM3R

vp2=3GM2R

va2=vp29=GM6R

Work done by gravity (perihelion to aphelion):

W=KEa−KEp=12mva2−12mvp2

=12m(GM6R−3GM2R)=12m⋅GM(16R−96R)

=GMm2⋅−86R=−2GMm3R

Magnitude =2GMm3R
!

questions.questionHtml: A man swims from a point A on one bank of a river of width 100 m. When he swims perpendicular to the water current, he reaches the other bank 50 m downstream. The angle to the bank at which he should swim, to reach the directly opposite point B on the other bank is
questions.solutionText: Solution Refer to figure (a), we know that here the ratio of vertical velocity and horizontal velocity gives the angle of resultant so, here v w = velocity of water in vertical direction , v m = velocity of man in horizontal direction Hence, tan θ = perpendicular distance horizontal distance = v w v m = 50 100 = 1 2 or v m = 2 v w Refer to figure (b), The angle to the bank at which he should swim, to reach the directly opposite point B on the other bank is given by, sin α = perpendicular distance hypotenuse distace = v w v m = v m 2 v m = 1 2 or α = 30 ° So, it is 90 ° - 30 ° = 60 ° upstream. ^

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Refer to figure (a), we know that here the ratio of vertical velocity and horizontal velocity gives the angle of resultant so, here

vw=velocity of water in vertical direction,

vm=velocity of man in horizontal direction

Hence,

tanθ=perpendicular distancehorizontal distance=vwvm=50100=12 or vm=2vw

Refer to figure (b), The angle to the bank at which he should swim, to reach the directly opposite point B on the other bank is given by,

sinα=perpendicular distancehypotenuse distace =vwvm=vm2vm=12 or α=30°

So, it is 90°-30°=60° upstream.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A simple pendulum with a solid metal bob of specific gravity 3 has a time period T 0 when suspended from the ceiling of a stationary elevator in air. The elevator then begins to accelerate downwards with a constant acceleration of a = g 3 . Simultaneously, the pendulum is completely immersed in a large tank of water (specific gravity 1) kept inside the elevator. What is the new time period of small oscillations of the pendulum? (Assume the water is completely non-viscous).
questions.questionText: A simple pendulum with a solid metal bob of specific gravity 3 has a time period T 0 when suspended from the ceiling of a stationary elevator in air. The elevator then begins to accelerate downwards with a constant acceleration of a = g 3 . questions.questionHtml: A simple pendulum with a solid metal bob of specific gravity 3 has a time period T0 when suspended from the ceiling of a stationary elevator in air. The elevator then begins to accelerate downwards with a constant acceleration of a=g3. Simultaneously, the pendulum is completely immersed in a large tank of water (specific gravity 1) kept inside the elevator. What is the new time period of small oscillations of the pendulum? (Assume the water is completely non-viscous).
questions.solutionText: Solution Specific gravity of bob = 3, so ρ b o b = 3 ρ w In the original case (stationary elevator, in air): T 0 = 2 π l g Now: elevator accelerates downward with a = g 3 , and pendulum is immersed in water. Effective gravity in elevator (pseudo force acts upward): g e f f = g − a = g − g 3 = 2 g 3 Buoyant force reduces effective weight. Effective gravitational acceleration for immersed pendulum: g ′ = g e f f ( 1 − ρ w ρ b o b ) = 2 g 3 ( 1 − 1 3 ) = 2 g 3 ⋅ 2 3 = 4 g 9 New time period: T = 2 π l g ′ = 2 π l 4 g 9 = 2 π 9 l 4 g = 3 2 ⋅ 2 π l g = 3 2 T 0 :
questions.solution: Solution Specific gravity of bob = 3, so ρ b o b = 3 ρ w In the original case (stationary elevator, in air): T 0 = 2 π l g Now: elevator accelerates downward with a = g 3 , and pendulum is immersed in water. Effective gravity in elevator (pseudo force acts upward): g e f f = g − a = g − g 3 = 2 g 3 Buoyant force reduces effective weight. Effective gravitational acceleration for immersed pendulum: g ′ = g e f f ( 1 − ρ w ρ b o b ) = 2 g 3 ( 1 − 1 3 ) = 2 g 3 ⋅ 2 3 = 4 g 9 New time period: T = 2 π l g ′ = 2 π l 4 g 9 = 2 π 9 l 4 g = 3 2 ⋅ 2 π l g = 3 2 T 0 :
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Specific gravity of bob = 3, so ρbob=3ρw

In the original case (stationary elevator, in air):
T0=2πlg

Now: elevator accelerates downward with a=g3, and pendulum is immersed in water.

Effective gravity in elevator (pseudo force acts upward):
geff=g−a=g−g3=2g3

Buoyant force reduces effective weight. Effective gravitational acceleration for immersed pendulum:
g′=geff(1−ρwρbob)=2g3(1−13)=2g3⋅23=4g9

New time period:
T=2πlg′=2πl4g9=2π9l4g=32⋅2πlg=32T0
:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A Carnot engine operating between a source at temperature T 1 and a sink at temperature T 2 has an efficiency of 40 %. If the sink temperature is decreased by 40 K while keeping the source temperature constant, the efficiency of the engine increases to 50 %. What is the temperature of the source ( T 1 )?
questions.questionText: A Carnot engine operating between a source at temperature T 1 and a sink at temperature T 2 has an efficiency of 40 %. If the sink temperature is decreased by 40 K while keeping the source temperature constant, the efficiency of the engine increases to 50 %. What is the temperature of the source ( T 1 )?
questions.question: A Carnot engine operating between a source at temperature T 1 and a sink at temperature T 2 has an efficiency of 40 %. If the sink temperature is decreased by 40 K while keeping the source temperature constant, the efficiency of the engine increases to 50 %. What is the temperature of the source ( T 1 )?
questions.prompt: A Carnot engine operating between a source at temperature T 1 and a sink at temperature T 2 has an efficiency of 40 %. If the sink temperature is decreased by 40 K while keeping the source temperature constant, the efficiency of the engine increases to 50 %. What is the temperature of the source ( T 1 )?
questions.html: A Carnot engine operating between a source at temperature T1 and a sink at temperature T2 has an efficiency of 40%. If the sink temperature is decreased by 40 K while keeping the source temperature constant, the efficiency of the engine increases to 50%. What is the temperature of the source (T1)?
questions.questionHtml: A Carnot engine operating between a source at temperature T1 and a sink at temperature T2 has an efficiency of 40%. If the sink temperature is decreased by 40 K while keeping the source temperature constant, the efficiency of the engine increases to 50%. What is the temperature of the source (T1)?
questions.solutionText: Solution Carnot efficiency: η = 1 − T 2 T 1 Case 1: 0.4 = 1 − T 2 T 1 ⇒ T 2 T 1 = 0.6 ⇒ T 2 = 0.6 T 1 Case 2: 0.5 = 1 − T 2 − 40 T 1 ⇒ T 2 − 40 T 1 = 0.5 Substituting T 2 = 0.6 T 1 : 0.6 T 1 − 40 T 1 = 0.5 0.6 T 1 − 40 = 0.5 T 1 0.1 T 1 = 40 T 1 = 400 K
questions.solution: Solution Carnot efficiency: η = 1 − T 2 T 1 Case 1: 0.4 = 1 − T 2 T 1 ⇒ T 2 T 1 = 0.6 ⇒ T 2 = 0.6 T 1 Case 2: 0.5 = 1 − T 2 − 40 T 1 ⇒ T 2 − 40 T 1 = 0.5 Substituting T 2 = 0.6 T 1 : 0.6 T 1 − 40 T 1 = 0.5 0.6 T 1 − 40 = 0.5 T 1 0.1 T 1 = 40 T 1 = 400 K
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Carnot efficiency: η=1−T2T1

Case 1: 0.4=1−T2T1⇒T2T1=0.6⇒T2=0.6T1

Case 2: 0.5=1−T2−40T1⇒T2−40T1=0.5

Substituting T2=0.6T1:
0.6T1−40T1=0.5

0.6T1−40=0.5T1

0.1T1=40

T1=400 K

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A flatcar (trolley) weighing 400 kg is moving on frictionless horizontal rails at a constant speed of 2 m/s . A man weighing 100 kg is standing at the extreme front end of the flatcar. He starts running towards the rear end with a constant acceleration of 1 m/s 2 relative to the flatcar. If the flatcar is 8 m long, what is the total displacement of the man relative to the ground by the time he reaches the rear end?

He starts running towards the rear end with a constant acceleration of 1 m/s2 relative to the flatcar. If the flatcar is 8 m long, what is the total displacement of the man relative to the ground by the time he reaches the rear end?
questions.questionHtml: A flatcar (trolley) weighing 400 kg is moving on frictionless horizontal rails at a constant speed of 2 m/s. A man weighing 100 kg is standing at the extreme front end of the flatcar. He starts running towards the rear end with a constant acceleration of 1 m/s2 relative to the flatcar. If the flatcar is 8 m long, what is the total displacement of the man relative to the ground by the time he reaches the rear end?
questions.solutionText: Solution Man moves 8 m relative to flatcar. Time taken: 8 = 1 2 ( 1 ) t 2 ⇒ t = 4 s No external horizontal force ⇒ v c m = 2 m / s (constant) Displacement of CM in 4 s : x c m = 2 × 4 = 8 m Let displacement of flatcar = x f , man = x m (relative to ground). Man moves 8 m backward relative to flatcar: x m = x f − 8 400 ⋅ x f + 100 ⋅ x m 500 = 8 400 x f + 100 ( x f − 8 ) = 4000 500 x f = 4800 ⇒ x f = 9.6 m x m = 9.6 − 8 = 1.6 m
questions.solution: Solution Man moves 8 m relative to flatcar. Time taken: 8 = 1 2 ( 1 ) t 2 ⇒ t = 4 s No external horizontal force ⇒ v c m = 2 m / s (constant) Displacement of CM in 4 s : x c m = 2 × 4 = 8 m Let displacement of flatcar = x f , man = x m (relative to ground). Man moves 8 m backward relative to flatcar: x m = x f − 8 400 ⋅ x f + 100 ⋅ x m 500 = 8 400 x f + 100 ( x f − 8 ) = 4000 500 x f = 4800 ⇒ x f = 9.6 m x m = 9.6 − 8 = 1.6 m
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Man moves 8m relative to flatcar. Time taken:

8=12(1)t2⇒t=4s

No external horizontal force ⇒ vcm=2m/s (constant)

Displacement of CM in 4s: xcm=2×4=8m

Let displacement of flatcar =xf, man =xm (relative to ground).

Man moves 8m backward relative to flatcar: xm=xf−8

400⋅xf+100⋅xm500=8

400xf+100(xf−8)=4000

500xf=4800⇒xf=9.6m

xm=9.6−8=1.6m

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two electromagnetic waves, wave X and wave Y , are propagating in a vacuum. The momentum of a single photon of wave X is exactly 10 4 times the momentum of a single photon of wave Y . If wave Y belongs to the visible light region of the electromagnetic spectrum (assume its wavelength is λ Y = 600 nm ), which region does wave X most likely belong to?
questions.questionText: Two electromagnetic waves, wave X and wave Y , are propagating in a vacuum. questions.questionHtml: Two electromagnetic waves, wave X and wave Y, are propagating in a vacuum. The momentum of a single photon of wave X is exactly 104 times the momentum of a single photon of wave Y. If wave Y belongs to the visible light region of the electromagnetic spectrum (assume its wavelength is λY=600 nm), which region does wave X most likely belong to?
questions.solutionText: Solution Momentum of a photon: p = h λ Given: p X = 10 4 ⋅ p Y h λ X = 10 4 ⋅ h λ Y λ X = λ Y 10 4 = 600 nm 10 4 = 0.06 nm = 6 × 10 − 11 m This wavelength falls in the X-rays region.
questions.solution: Solution Momentum of a photon: p = h λ Given: p X = 10 4 ⋅ p Y h λ X = 10 4 ⋅ h λ Y λ X = λ Y 10 4 = 600 nm 10 4 = 0.06 nm = 6 × 10 − 11 m This wavelength falls in the X-rays region.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Momentum of a photon: p=hλ

Given: pX=104⋅pY

hλX=104⋅hλY

λX=λY104=600 nm104=0.06 nm=6×10−11 m

This wavelength falls in the X-rays region.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The steady-state amplitude A of a forced, damped harmonic oscillator is given by the expression: A = 50 2 ω 4 − 36 ω 2 + 200 where ω is the driving angular frequency in rad/s. At what driving frequency does resonance (maximum amplitude) occur?

questions.solutionText: Solution A = 50 2 ω 4 − 36 ω 2 + 200 For maximum A , minimize f ( ω ) = 2 ω 4 − 36 ω 2 + 200 d f d ω = 8 ω 3 − 72 ω = 0 8 ω ( ω 2 − 9 ) = 0 ω = 0 or ω 2 = 9 ⇒ ω = 3 rad/s
questions.solution: Solution A = 50 2 ω 4 − 36 ω 2 + 200 For maximum A , minimize f ( ω ) = 2 ω 4 − 36 ω 2 + 200 d f d ω = 8 ω 3 − 72 ω = 0 8 ω ( ω 2 − 9 ) = 0 ω = 0 or ω 2 = 9 ⇒ ω = 3 rad/s

Solution
A=502ω4−36ω2+200

For maximum A, minimize f(ω)=2ω4−36ω2+200

dfdω=8ω3−72ω=0

8ω(ω2−9)=0

ω=0 or ω2=9⇒ω=3 rad/s

questions.solutionText: Solution We knows frequency of a closed end an column n 1 = v 4 / 1 We knows frequency of a open end an column n 2 = v 2 l 2 Given, l 1 = 32 cm , I 2 = 66 cm and n 1 − n 2 = 8 heat / s So, n 1 = v 4 × 32 = v 128 and n 2 = v 2 × 66 = v 132 In given condition, v 128 − v 132 = 8 v = 8448 × 4 v = 33792 Hence, n 1 = 33792 128 n 1 = 264 Hz and n 2 = 33792 132 n 2 = 256 Hz

questions.question: Among the following, the main reactions occurring in blast furnace during extraction of iron from haematite are: i. Fe 2 O 3 + 3 CO → 2 Fe + 3 CO 2 ii. FeO + SiO 2 → FeSiO 3 iii. Fe 2 O 3 + 3 C → 2 Fe + 3 CO iv. CaO + SiO 2 → CaSiO 3
questions.prompt: Among the following, the main reactions occurring in blast furnace during extraction of iron from haematite are: i. Fe 2 O 3 + 3 CO → 2 Fe + 3 CO 2 ii. FeO + SiO 2 → FeSiO 3 iii. Fe 2 O 3 + 3 C → 2 Fe + 3 CO iv. CaO + SiO 2 → CaSiO 3
questions.html:
Among the following, the main reactions occurring in blast furnace during extraction of iron from haematite are:
i. Fe2O3+3CO→2Fe+3CO2
ii. FeO+SiO2→FeSiO3
iii. Fe2O3+3C→2Fe+3CO
iv. CaO+SiO2→CaSiO3
questions.questionHtml:
Among the following, the main reactions occurring in blast furnace during extraction of iron from haematite are:
i. Fe2O3+3CO→2Fe+3CO2
ii. FeO+SiO2→FeSiO3
iii. Fe2O3+3C→2Fe+3CO
iv. CaO+SiO2→CaSiO3
questions.solutionText: Solution In a blast furnace during extraction of iron from haematite ( F e 2 O 3 ): i. F e 2 O 3 + 3 C O → 2 F e + 3 C O 2 → This is the main reduction reaction in the blast furnace. C O (produced from coke) acts as the reducing agent. This is correct. ii. F e O + S i O 2 → F e S i O 3 → This would mean iron oxide acts as a flux. This is not desirable as it leads to loss of iron. Not a main reaction. iii. F e 2 O 3 + 3 C → 2 F e + 3 C O → Direct reduction by carbon. This is not the main reaction; reduction is primarily done by C O , not directly by C . iv. C a O + S i O 2 → C a S i O 3 → This is the slag formation reaction. C a O (from limestone flux) combines with the gangue S i O 2 to form calcium silicate slag. This is correct.
questions.solution: Solution In a blast furnace during extraction of iron from haematite ( F e 2 O 3 ): i. F e 2 O 3 + 3 C O → 2 F e + 3 C O 2 → This is the main reduction reaction in the blast furnace. C O (produced from coke) acts as the reducing agent. This is correct. ii. F e O + S i O 2 → F e S i O 3 → This would mean iron oxide acts as a flux. This is not desirable as it leads to loss of iron. Not a main reaction. iii. F e 2 O 3 + 3 C → 2 F e + 3 C O → Direct reduction by carbon. This is not the main reaction; reduction is primarily done by C O , not directly by C . iv. C a O + S i O 2 → C a S i O 3 → This is the slag formation reaction. C a O (from limestone flux) combines with the gangue S i O 2 to form calcium silicate slag. This is correct.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
In a blast furnace during extraction of iron from haematite (Fe2O3):

i. Fe2O3+3CO→2Fe+3CO2 → This is the main reduction reaction in the blast furnace. CO (produced from coke) acts as the reducing agent. This is correct.

ii. FeO+SiO2→FeSiO3 → This would mean iron oxide acts as a flux. This is not desirable as it leads to loss of iron. Not a main reaction.

iii. Fe2O3+3C→2Fe+3CO → Direct reduction by carbon. This is not the main reaction; reduction is primarily done by CO, not directly by C.

iv. CaO+SiO2→CaSiO3 → This is the slag formation reaction. CaO (from limestone flux) combines with the gangue SiO2 to form calcium silicate slag. This is correct.

questions.text: Brown haze produced in photochemical smog is due to:
questions.questionText: Brown haze produced in photochemical smog is due to:
questions.question: Brown haze produced in photochemical smog is due to:
questions.prompt: Brown haze produced in photochemical smog is due to:
questions.html: Brown haze produced in photochemical smog is due to:
questions.questionHtml: Brown haze produced in photochemical smog is due to:
questions.solutionText: Solution Photochemical smog is initiated by the photochemical dissociation of N O 2 and the resulting secondary reactions involving unsaturated hydrocarbons, other organic compounds and free radicals, leading to the formation of organic peroxides and ozone. Hydrocarbons + O 3 , O 2 , O , NO 2 , NO ; peroxides, peroxyacetyl nitrate, formaldehyde, ozone aldehyde, acrolein, etc. oxidised hydrocarbons and ozone in the presence of humidity cause photochemical smog, which dissipates at night.
questions.solution: Solution Photochemical smog is initiated by the photochemical dissociation of N O 2 and the resulting secondary reactions involving unsaturated hydrocarbons, other organic compounds and free radicals, leading to the formation of organic peroxides and ozone. Hydrocarbons + O 3 , O 2 , O , NO 2 , NO ; peroxides, peroxyacetyl nitrate, formaldehyde, ozone aldehyde, acrolein, etc. oxidised hydrocarbons and ozone in the presence of humidity cause photochemical smog, which dissipates at night.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Photochemical smog is initiated by the photochemical dissociation of NO2 and the resulting secondary reactions involving unsaturated hydrocarbons, other organic compounds and free radicals, leading to the formation of organic peroxides and ozone.



Hydrocarbons +O3, O2, O,NO2, NO; peroxides, peroxyacetyl nitrate, formaldehyde, ozone aldehyde, acrolein, etc. oxidised hydrocarbons and ozone in the presence of humidity cause photochemical smog, which dissipates at night.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which one of the following statements regarding the health effects of water pollutants is correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements regarding the health effects of water pollutants is correct?
questions.question: Which one of the following statements regarding the health effects of water pollutants is correct?
questions.prompt: Which one of the following statements regarding the health effects of water pollutants is correct?
questions.html: Which one of the following statements regarding the health effects of water pollutants is correct?
questions.questionHtml: Which one of the following statements regarding the health effects of water pollutants is correct?
questions.solutionText: Solution (1) Incorrect: Excess nitrate in drinking water causes Methemoglobinemia, commonly known as "Blue Baby Syndrome," which reduces the blood's ability to carry oxygen. Minamata disease is caused by methylmercury poisoning. (2) Correct: Long-term exposure to high levels of arsenic in drinking water is directly linked to Black foot disease, a severe disease of blood vessels leading to gangrene. (3) Incorrect: High concentrations of lead cause lead poisoning (plumbism), which damages the nervous system and kidneys. Itai-Itai disease is caused by Cadmium poisoning. (4) Incorrect: Excess fluoride in drinking water causes Fluorosis (mottling of teeth and skeletal issues). Methemoglobinemia is caused by excess nitrates.
questions.solution: Solution (1) Incorrect: Excess nitrate in drinking water causes Methemoglobinemia, commonly known as "Blue Baby Syndrome," which reduces the blood's ability to carry oxygen. Minamata disease is caused by methylmercury poisoning. (2) Correct: Long-term exposure to high levels of arsenic in drinking water is directly linked to Black foot disease, a severe disease of blood vessels leading to gangrene. (3) Incorrect: High concentrations of lead cause lead poisoning (plumbism), which damages the nervous system and kidneys. Itai-Itai disease is caused by Cadmium poisoning. (4) Incorrect: Excess fluoride in drinking water causes Fluorosis (mottling of teeth and skeletal issues). Methemoglobinemia is caused by excess nitrates.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(1) Incorrect: Excess nitrate in drinking water causes Methemoglobinemia, commonly known as "Blue Baby Syndrome," which reduces the blood's ability to carry oxygen. Minamata disease is caused by methylmercury poisoning.

(2) Correct: Long-term exposure to high levels of arsenic in drinking water is directly linked to Black foot disease, a severe disease of blood vessels leading to gangrene.

(3) Incorrect: High concentrations of lead cause lead poisoning (plumbism), which damages the nervous system and kidneys. Itai-Itai disease is caused by Cadmium poisoning.

(4) Incorrect: Excess fluoride in drinking water causes Fluorosis (mottling of teeth and skeletal issues). Methemoglobinemia is caused by excess nitrates.

Solution
Electronic configurations of the cations:

Mn2+: [Ar] 3d5 → 5 unpaired electrons

Ni2+: [Ar] 3d8 → 2 unpaired electrons

Mn2+ has the maximum (5) unpaired electrons due to its half-filled 3d subshell.

questions.prompt: What are X and Y in the following reactions? C H 2 O → (ii) H 3 O + (i) X C H 3 ( C H 2 ) 2 C H 2 O H Y → (ii) H 3 O + (i) C 2 H 5 M g B r C H 3 C H 2 C ( C H 3 ) 2 O H
questions.html:
What are X and Y in the following reactions?

CH2O→(ii) H3O+(i) XCH3(CH2)2CH2OH

Y→(ii) H3O+(i) C2H5MgBrCH3CH2C(CH3)2 OH
questions.questionHtml:
What are X and Y in the following reactions?

CH2O→(ii) H3O+(i) XCH3(CH2)2CH2OH

Y→(ii) H3O+(i) C2H5MgBrCH3CH2C(CH3)2 OH
questions.solutionText: Solution Reaction 1: C H 2 O → (ii) H 3 O + (i) X C H 3 ( C H 2 ) 2 C H 2 O H Product is C H 3 C H 2 C H 2 C H 2 O H (butan-1-ol, a primary alcohol with 4 carbons). C H 2 O (formaldehyde) has 1 carbon, so X must supply 3 carbons as a Grignard reagent. X = C H 3 C H 2 C H 2 M g B r (n-propyl magnesium bromide) Reaction 2: Y → (ii) H 3 O + (i) C 2 H 5 M g B r C H 3 C H 2 C ( C H 3 ) 2 O H Product is a tertiary alcohol with 5 carbons. C 2 H 5 M g B r supplies 2 carbons. So Y must have 3 carbons and produce a tertiary alcohol ⇒ Y must be a ketone. Y = C H 3 C O C H 3 (acetone) Grignard on acetone: C H 3 C O C H 3 + C 2 H 5 M g B r → C H 3 C H 2 C ( C H 3 ) 2 O H
questions.solution: Solution Reaction 1: C H 2 O → (ii) H 3 O + (i) X C H 3 ( C H 2 ) 2 C H 2 O H Product is C H 3 C H 2 C H 2 C H 2 O H (butan-1-ol, a primary alcohol with 4 carbons). C H 2 O (formaldehyde) has 1 carbon, so X must supply 3 carbons as a Grignard reagent. X = C H 3 C H 2 C H 2 M g B r (n-propyl magnesium bromide) Reaction 2: Y → (ii) H 3 O + (i) C 2 H 5 M g B r C H 3 C H 2 C ( C H 3 ) 2 O H Product is a tertiary alcohol with 5 carbons. C 2 H 5 M g B r supplies 2 carbons. So Y must have 3 carbons and produce a tertiary alcohol ⇒ Y must be a ketone. Y = C H 3 C O C H 3 (acetone) Grignard on acetone: C H 3 C O C H 3 + C 2 H 5 M g B r → C H 3 C H 2 C ( C H 3 ) 2 O H
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Reaction 1: CH2O→(ii) H3O+(i) XCH3(CH2)2CH2OH

Product is CH3CH2CH2CH2OH (butan-1-ol, a primary alcohol with 4 carbons).
CH2O (formaldehyde) has 1 carbon, so X must supply 3 carbons as a Grignard reagent.
X=CH3CH2CH2MgBr (n-propyl magnesium bromide)

Reaction 2: Y→(ii) H3O+(i) C2H5MgBrCH3CH2C(CH3)2OH

Product is a tertiary alcohol with 5 carbons. C2H5MgBr supplies 2 carbons.
So Y must have 3 carbons and produce a tertiary alcohol ⇒Y must be a ketone.
Y=CH3COCH3 (acetone)

Grignard on acetone: CH3COCH3+C2H5MgBr→CH3CH2C(CH3)2OH

Which set of the terms correctly identifies the carbohydrate shown?

(i) Pentose (ii) Hexose (iii) Aldose

(iv) Ketose (v) Pyranose (vi) Furanose
questions.questionHtml:
Which set of the terms correctly identifies the carbohydrate shown?

(i) Pentose (ii) Hexose (iii) Aldose

(iv) Ketose (v) Pyranose (vi) Furanose
questions.solutionText: Solution A hexose is used to define a carbohydrate with six carbons. A polyhydroxy compound with ketone group is known as ketose. A cyclic structure resembling pyran ring is known as pyranose. Aldose is used for carbohydrate having aldehyde functional group. The word pentose is used for carbohydrate having 5 carbon atoms. The word furanose is used for carbohydrate having structure like furan. The above structure contain 5 -carbon atoms, aldehyde functional group, and it is furan like structure.
questions.solution: Solution A hexose is used to define a carbohydrate with six carbons. A polyhydroxy compound with ketone group is known as ketose. A cyclic structure resembling pyran ring is known as pyranose. Aldose is used for carbohydrate having aldehyde functional group. The word pentose is used for carbohydrate having 5 carbon atoms. The word furanose is used for carbohydrate having structure like furan. The above structure contain 5 -carbon atoms, aldehyde functional group, and it is furan like structure.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

A hexose is used to define a carbohydrate with six carbons.

A polyhydroxy compound with ketone group is known as ketose.

A cyclic structure resembling pyran ring is known as pyranose.

Aldose is used for carbohydrate having aldehyde functional group.

The word pentose is used for carbohydrate having 5 carbon atoms.

The word furanose is used for carbohydrate having structure like furan.

The above structure contain 5-carbon atoms, aldehyde functional group, and it is furan like structure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Identify the compound which turns black with ammonia solution.
questions.questionText: Identify the compound which turns black with ammonia solution.
questions.question: Identify the compound which turns black with ammonia solution.
questions.prompt: Identify the compound which turns black with ammonia solution.
questions.html: Identify the compound which turns black with ammonia solution.
questions.questionHtml: Identify the compound which turns black with ammonia solution.
questions.solutionText: Solution Hg + 2 , Pb + 2 , Ag + lie in the first group. Qualitative analysis of group one cations is done via primary test i.e., by the addition of HCl in cold water. Formation of white precipitate confirms the presence of first group. Mercurous ion exists as a diatomic ion and precipitates as white coloured mercurous chloride on reaction with HCl . Hg 2 2 + ⟶ Dil HCl Hg 2 Cl 2 ↓ white This Hg 2 Cl 2 reacts with ammonia to form black precipitate of mercury metal along with white coloured mercury amidochloride and the black precipitate dominates its colour. Hg 2 Cl 2 ⟶ NH 3 aq Hg ↓ black + Hg NH 2 Cl ↓ white ⏟ Black
questions.solution: Solution Hg + 2 , Pb + 2 , Ag + lie in the first group. Qualitative analysis of group one cations is done via primary test i.e., by the addition of HCl in cold water. Formation of white precipitate confirms the presence of first group. Mercurous ion exists as a diatomic ion and precipitates as white coloured mercurous chloride on reaction with HCl . Hg 2 2 + ⟶ Dil HCl Hg 2 Cl 2 ↓ white This Hg 2 Cl 2 reacts with ammonia to form black precipitate of mercury metal along with white coloured mercury amidochloride and the black precipitate dominates its colour. Hg 2 Cl 2 ⟶ NH 3 aq Hg ↓ black + Hg NH 2 Cl ↓ white ⏟ Black
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Hg+2, Pb+2, Ag+ lie in the first group. Qualitative analysis of group one cations is done via primary test i.e., by the addition of HCl in cold water. Formation of white precipitate confirms the presence of first group.

Mercurous ion exists as a diatomic ion and precipitates as white coloured mercurous chloride on reaction with HCl.Hg22+⟶Dil HClHg2Cl2↓white

This Hg2Cl2 reacts with ammonia to form black precipitate of mercury metal along with white coloured mercury amidochloride and the black precipitate dominates its colour.

Hg2Cl2⟶NH3aqHg↓black+HgNH2Cl↓white⏟Black

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The first ionization energy ( I E 1 ) and the electron affinity ( E A ) of a certain non-metal element X are experimentally determined to be 13.0 eV/atom and 3.8 eV/atom , respectively. What is the approximate electronegativity of element X on the widely used Pauling scale?
questions.questionText: The first ionization energy ( I E 1 ) and the electron affinity ( E A ) of a certain non-metal element X are experimentally determined to be 13.0 eV/atom and 3.8 eV/atom , respectively. What is the approximate electronegativity of element X on the widely used Pauling scale?
questions.question: The first ionization energy ( I E 1 ) and the electron affinity ( E A ) of a certain non-metal element X are experimentally determined to be 13.0 eV/atom and 3.8 eV/atom , respectively. What is the approximate electronegativity of element X on the widely used Pauling scale?
questions.prompt: The first ionization energy ( I E 1 ) and the electron affinity ( E A ) of a certain non-metal element X are experimentally determined to be 13.0 eV/atom and 3.8 eV/atom , respectively. What is the approximate electronegativity of element X on the widely used Pauling scale?
questions.html: The first ionization energy (IE1) and the electron affinity (EA) of a certain non-metal element X are experimentally determined to be 13.0 eV/atom and 3.8 eV/atom, respectively. What is the approximate electronegativity of element X on the widely used Pauling scale?
questions.questionHtml: The first ionization energy (IE1) and the electron affinity (EA) of a certain non-metal element X are experimentally determined to be 13.0 eV/atom and 3.8 eV/atom, respectively. What is the approximate electronegativity of element X on the widely used Pauling scale?
questions.solutionText: Solution Mulliken's electronegativity: χ M = I E + E A 2 = 13.0 + 3.8 2 = 8.4 eV Converting to Pauling scale: χ P = χ M 2.8 = 8.4 2.8 = 3.0 The approximate electronegativity of element X on the Pauling scale is 3.0 .
questions.solution: Solution Mulliken's electronegativity: χ M = I E + E A 2 = 13.0 + 3.8 2 = 8.4 eV Converting to Pauling scale: χ P = χ M 2.8 = 8.4 2.8 = 3.0 The approximate electronegativity of element X on the Pauling scale is 3.0 .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Mulliken's electronegativity:
χM=IE+EA2=13.0+3.82=8.4 eV

Converting to Pauling scale:
χP=χM2.8=8.42.8=3.0

The approximate electronegativity of element X on the Pauling scale is 3.0.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: It is easier to liquefy ammonia than oxygen because
questions.questionText: It is easier to liquefy ammonia than oxygen because
questions.question: It is easier to liquefy ammonia than oxygen because
questions.prompt: It is easier to liquefy ammonia than oxygen because
questions.html: It is easier to liquefy ammonia than oxygen because
questions.questionHtml: It is easier to liquefy ammonia than oxygen because
questions.solutionText: Solution N H 3 has stronger intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonding) compared to O 2 (weak van der Waals forces). This gives N H 3 a higher value of van der Waals constant a (which measures intermolecular attraction) and consequently a higher critical temperature. A higher critical temperature means the gas can be liquefied more easily as it can be liquefied at relatively higher temperatures.
questions.solution: Solution N H 3 has stronger intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonding) compared to O 2 (weak van der Waals forces). This gives N H 3 a higher value of van der Waals constant a (which measures intermolecular attraction) and consequently a higher critical temperature. A higher critical temperature means the gas can be liquefied more easily as it can be liquefied at relatively higher temperatures.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
NH3 has stronger intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonding) compared to O2 (weak van der Waals forces). This gives NH3 a higher value of van der Waals constant a (which measures intermolecular attraction) and consequently a higher critical temperature. A higher critical temperature means the gas can be liquefied more easily as it can be liquefied at relatively higher temperatures.

questions.solutionText: Solution Relative abundance: It is the existence of a naturally occurring element in a percentage of atoms with a particular atomic weight or molar mass. There are three isotopes of hydrogen: Protium H , deuterium D and tritium T . Order of abundance: H > D > T Order of density: H < D < T Order of boiling point: H < D < T Order of atomic mass: H < D < T

Relative abundance:

It is the existence of a naturally occurring element in a percentage of atoms with a particular atomic weight or molar mass.

Protium H, deuterium D and tritium T.

Order of abundance: H>D>T

Order of density: H<D<T

Order of atomic mass: H<D<T

questions.text: The correct order of ionic size in polar solvent is
questions.questionText: The correct order of ionic size in polar solvent is
questions.question: The correct order of ionic size in polar solvent is
questions.prompt: The correct order of ionic size in polar solvent is
questions.html: The correct order of ionic size in polar solvent is
questions.questionHtml: The correct order of ionic size in polar solvent is
questions.solutionText: Solution In polar solvent, solvation is maximum for L i + , due to high charge density on small size. Hence size of solvated cation is in the order L i + > N a + > K + > R b +
questions.solution: Solution In polar solvent, solvation is maximum for L i + , due to high charge density on small size. Hence size of solvated cation is in the order L i + > N a + > K + > R b +
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
In polar solvent, solvation is maximum for Li+ , due to high charge density on small size. Hence size of solvated cation is in the order Li+>Na+>K+>Rb+

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When phosphorus trichloride ( P C l 3 ) undergoes complete hydrolysis, it yields a specific oxoacid of phosphorus. What is the basicity and the oxidation state of the central phosphorus atom in this resulting oxoacid, respectively?
questions.questionText: When phosphorus trichloride ( P C l 3 ) undergoes complete hydrolysis, it yields a specific oxoacid of phosphorus. What is the basicity and the oxidation state of the central phosphorus atom in this resulting oxoacid, respectively?
questions.question: When phosphorus trichloride ( P C l 3 ) undergoes complete hydrolysis, it yields a specific oxoacid of phosphorus. What is the basicity and the oxidation state of the central phosphorus atom in this resulting oxoacid, respectively?
questions.prompt: When phosphorus trichloride ( P C l 3 ) undergoes complete hydrolysis, it yields a specific oxoacid of phosphorus. What is the basicity and the oxidation state of the central phosphorus atom in this resulting oxoacid, respectively?
questions.html: When phosphorus trichloride (PCl3) undergoes complete hydrolysis, it yields a specific oxoacid of phosphorus. What is the basicity and the oxidation state of the central phosphorus atom in this resulting oxoacid, respectively?
questions.questionHtml: When phosphorus trichloride (PCl3) undergoes complete hydrolysis, it yields a specific oxoacid of phosphorus. What is the basicity and the oxidation state of the central phosphorus atom in this resulting oxoacid, respectively?
questions.solutionText: Solution P C l 3 + 3 H 2 O → H 3 P O 3 + 3 H C l The product is phosphorous acid ( H 3 P O 3 ). Structure: H 3 P O 3 has two O − H groups and one P − H bond, so only 2 replaceable hydrogen atoms. Basicity = number of ionizable O − H bonds = 2 Oxidation state of P in H 3 P O 3 : Let oxidation state of P = x x + 3 ( + 1 ) + 3 ( − 2 ) = 0 x + 3 − 6 = 0 ⇒ x = + 3
questions.solution: Solution P C l 3 + 3 H 2 O → H 3 P O 3 + 3 H C l The product is phosphorous acid ( H 3 P O 3 ). Structure: H 3 P O 3 has two O − H groups and one P − H bond, so only 2 replaceable hydrogen atoms. Basicity = number of ionizable O − H bonds = 2 Oxidation state of P in H 3 P O 3 : Let oxidation state of P = x x + 3 ( + 1 ) + 3 ( − 2 ) = 0 x + 3 − 6 = 0 ⇒ x = + 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
PCl3+3H2O→H3PO3+3HCl

The product is phosphorous acid (H3PO3).

Structure: H3PO3 has two O−H groups and one P−H bond, so only 2 replaceable hydrogen atoms.

Basicity = number of ionizable O−H bonds = 2

Oxidation state of P in H3PO3:
Let oxidation state of P = x
x+3(+1)+3(−2)=0
x+3−6=0⇒x=+3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carbocation which is most stable
questions.questionText: Carbocation which is most stable
questions.question: Carbocation which is most stable
questions.prompt: Carbocation which is most stable
questions.html: Carbocation which is most stable
questions.questionHtml: Carbocation which is most stable
questions.solutionText: Solution (1) C H 3 C H 2 + → primary carbocation (2) C H 3 + → methyl carbocation (3) C 6 H 5 C H 2 + → benzylic carbocation (stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring) (4) C H 3 C H 2 C H 2 + → primary carbocation The benzylic carbocation is stabilized by extensive resonance delocalization of the positive charge into the aromatic ring, making it far more stable than any of the primary or methyl carbocations listed.
questions.solution: Solution (1) C H 3 C H 2 + → primary carbocation (2) C H 3 + → methyl carbocation (3) C 6 H 5 C H 2 + → benzylic carbocation (stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring) (4) C H 3 C H 2 C H 2 + → primary carbocation The benzylic carbocation is stabilized by extensive resonance delocalization of the positive charge into the aromatic ring, making it far more stable than any of the primary or methyl carbocations listed.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(1) CH3CH2+ → primary carbocation
(2) CH3+ → methyl carbocation
(3) C6H5CH2+ → benzylic carbocation (stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring)
(4) CH3CH2CH2+ → primary carbocation

The benzylic carbocation is stabilized by extensive resonance delocalization of the positive charge into the aromatic ring, making it far more stable than any of the primary or methyl carbocations listed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is responsible for the depletion of the ozone layer in the upper strata of the atmosphere?

questions.prompt: Which of the following is responsible for the depletion of the ozone layer in the upper strata of the atmosphere?
questions.html: Which of the following is responsible for the depletion of the ozone layer in the upper strata of the atmosphere?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following is responsible for the depletion of the ozone layer in the upper strata of the atmosphere?
questions.solutionText: Solution Freons (chlorofluorocarbons / CFCs) are responsible for ozone layer depletion. They release chlorine radicals in the upper atmosphere upon UV exposure, which catalytically destroy ozone molecules.
questions.solution: Solution Freons (chlorofluorocarbons / CFCs) are responsible for ozone layer depletion. They release chlorine radicals in the upper atmosphere upon UV exposure, which catalytically destroy ozone molecules.

questions.solutionText: Solution A g ⟶ 2 B g ; k 1 = 6 . 9 3 × 1 0 - 3 min -1 A g ⟶ C g ; k 2 = 6 . 9 3 × 1 0 - 3 min -1 k = ( k 1 + k 2 ) overall velocity constant = 6.93 x 2 x 10 -3 min -1 t 1 / 2 = 0 . 6 9 3 × 1 0 0 0 6 . 9 3 × 2 = 5 0 min i.e., after = 50 min P A = 1 atm. questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which statements are incorrect about transition elements?
questions.questionText: Which statements are incorrect about transition elements?
questions.question: Which statements are incorrect about transition elements?
questions.prompt: Which statements are incorrect about transition elements?
questions.html: Which statements are incorrect about transition elements?
questions.questionHtml: Which statements are incorrect about transition elements?
questions.solutionText: Solution (1) They show variable valency → Correct. Transition elements exhibit multiple oxidation states. (2) They readily form complex compounds → Correct. Due to small size, high charge, and availability of vacant d-orbitals. (3) All their ions are colourless → Incorrect. Most transition metal ions are coloured due to d-d transitions. Only ions with d 0 or d 10 configurations are colourless. (4) Their ions contain partially filled d-orbitals → Correct. This is the defining characteristic of transition elements.
questions.solution: Solution (1) They show variable valency → Correct. Transition elements exhibit multiple oxidation states. (2) They readily form complex compounds → Correct. Due to small size, high charge, and availability of vacant d-orbitals. (3) All their ions are colourless → Incorrect. Most transition metal ions are coloured due to d-d transitions. Only ions with d 0 or d 10 configurations are colourless. (4) Their ions contain partially filled d-orbitals → Correct. This is the defining characteristic of transition elements.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(1) They show variable valency → Correct. Transition elements exhibit multiple oxidation states.

(2) They readily form complex compounds → Correct. Due to small size, high charge, and availability of vacant d-orbitals.

(3) All their ions are colourless → Incorrect. Most transition metal ions are coloured due to d-d transitions. Only ions with d0 or d10 configurations are colourless.

(4) Their ions contain partially filled d-orbitals → Correct. This is the defining characteristic of transition elements.

questions.text: The following reactions are carried out on para-cresol (4-methylphenol): 4-methylphenol → B r 2 , H 2 O A 4-methylphenol → B r 2 , C S 2 B Identify the major products A and B respectively:
questions.questionText: The following reactions are carried out on para-cresol (4-methylphenol): 4-methylphenol → B r 2 , H 2 O A 4-methylphenol → B r 2 , C S 2 B Identify the major products A and B respectively:
questions.question: The following reactions are carried out on para-cresol (4-methylphenol): 4-methylphenol → B r 2 , H 2 O A 4-methylphenol → B r 2 , C S 2 B Identify the major products A and B respectively:
questions.prompt: The following reactions are carried out on para-cresol (4-methylphenol): 4-methylphenol → B r 2 , H 2 O A 4-methylphenol → B r 2 , C S 2 B Identify the major products A and B respectively:
questions.html:
The following reactions are carried out on para-cresol (4-methylphenol):

4-methylphenol →Br2, H2O A

4-methylphenol →Br2, CS2 B

Identify the major products A and B respectively:
questions.questionHtml:
The following reactions are carried out on para-cresol (4-methylphenol):

4-methylphenol →Br2, H2O A

4-methylphenol →Br2, CS2 B

Identify the major products A and B respectively:
questions.solutionText: Solution The starting compound is para-cresol (4-methylphenol). − O H is a strong activating group (ortho/para director), and − C H 3 is also activating (ortho/para director). Both groups are para to each other. Available positions for electrophilic substitution: ortho to − O H (positions 2 and 6). With B r 2 / H 2 O (aqueous bromine): − O H group in aqueous medium is highly activated. Bromine water is a strong brominating agent for phenols, leading to bromination at all available ortho and para positions relative to − O H . Since para is blocked by − C H 3 , both ortho positions (2 and 6) get brominated. A = 2,6-dibromo-4-methylphenol With B r 2 / C S 2 (bromine in non-polar solvent): C S 2 is a non-polar solvent, and this is a milder brominating condition. Only mono-bromination occurs at the most activated ortho position relative to − O H . B = 2-bromo-4-methylphenol
questions.solution: Solution The starting compound is para-cresol (4-methylphenol). − O H is a strong activating group (ortho/para director), and − C H 3 is also activating (ortho/para director). Both groups are para to each other. Available positions for electrophilic substitution: ortho to − O H (positions 2 and 6). With B r 2 / H 2 O (aqueous bromine): − O H group in aqueous medium is highly activated. Bromine water is a strong brominating agent for phenols, leading to bromination at all available ortho and para positions relative to − O H . Since para is blocked by − C H 3 , both ortho positions (2 and 6) get brominated. A = 2,6-dibromo-4-methylphenol With B r 2 / C S 2 (bromine in non-polar solvent): C S 2 is a non-polar solvent, and this is a milder brominating condition. Only mono-bromination occurs at the most activated ortho position relative to − O H . B = 2-bromo-4-methylphenol
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The starting compound is para-cresol (4-methylphenol). −OH is a strong activating group (ortho/para director), and −CH3 is also activating (ortho/para director). Both groups are para to each other.

Available positions for electrophilic substitution: ortho to −OH (positions 2 and 6).

With Br2/H2O (aqueous bromine):
−OH group in aqueous medium is highly activated. Bromine water is a strong brominating agent for phenols, leading to bromination at all available ortho and para positions relative to −OH. Since para is blocked by −CH3, both ortho positions (2 and 6) get brominated.

A = 2,6-dibromo-4-methylphenol

With Br2/CS2 (bromine in non-polar solvent):
CS2 is a non-polar solvent, and this is a milder brominating condition. Only mono-bromination occurs at the most activated ortho position relative to −OH.

B = 2-bromo-4-methylphenol

questions.questionText: The tertiary structure of proteins can be destroyed by
questions.question: The tertiary structure of proteins can be destroyed by
questions.prompt: The tertiary structure of proteins can be destroyed by
questions.html: The tertiary structure of proteins can be destroyed by
questions.questionHtml: The tertiary structure of proteins can be destroyed by
questions.solutionText: Solution Tertiary structures can usually be destroyed (called denaturation) by heating a protein, treating with high energy radiation, or changing the solvent environment (pH, salt content, organic content, etc.) in which the protein is dissolved. When the characteristic structure of a protein molecule in its physiological environment is lost, the protein's normal function is lost too.

questions.solutionText: Solution The Ziegler-Natta catalyst ( T i C l 4 + A l ( C 2 H 5 ) 3 ) is specifically used for the stereospecific polymerisation of olefins (alkenes) such as ethylene and propylene to produce high-density polyethylene and isotactic polypropylene.
questions.solution: Solution The Ziegler-Natta catalyst ( T i C l 4 + A l ( C 2 H 5 ) 3 ) is specifically used for the stereospecific polymerisation of olefins (alkenes) such as ethylene and propylene to produce high-density polyethylene and isotactic polypropylene.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The Ziegler-Natta catalyst (TiCl4+Al(C2H5)3) is specifically used for the stereospecific polymerisation of olefins (alkenes) such as ethylene and propylene to produce high-density polyethylene and isotactic polypropylene.

questions.html: The solubility product of Sb2S3 is experimentally determined to be 1.08×10−23 at 298 K. What is the molar solubility of this sulphide precipitate in pure water?
questions.questionHtml: The solubility product of Sb2S3 is experimentally determined to be 1.08×10−23 at 298 K. What is the molar solubility of this sulphide precipitate in pure water?
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Sb2S3⇌2Sb3++3S2−

Let molar solubility = s

[Sb3+]=2s, [S2−]=3s

Ksp=(2s)2(3s)3=4s2⋅27s3=108s5

1.08×10−23=108s5

s5=1.08×10−23108=1.0×10−25

s=(10−25)1/5=10−5 mol/L

questions.solutionText: Solution Laconic: using very few words; brief and terse in speech or expression. (1) Verbose: using more words than necessary; excessively wordy. (Opposite of laconic) (2) Concise: expressing much in few words; brief but comprehensive. (Closest in meaning to laconic) (3) Confused: unable to think clearly; lacking order or clarity. (Unrelated to laconic) (4) Elaborate: detailed and carefully prepared; involving many parts or complexities. (Opposite of laconic)

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Laconic: using very few words; brief and terse in speech or expression.

(1) Verbose: using more words than necessary; excessively wordy. (Opposite of laconic)

(2) Concise: expressing much in few words; brief but comprehensive. (Closest in meaning to laconic)

(3) Confused: unable to think clearly; lacking order or clarity. (Unrelated to laconic)

(4) Elaborate: detailed and carefully prepared; involving many parts or complexities. (Opposite of laconic)

questions.questionHtml:
In the question given below, a sentence with a blank is given. Choose the alternative that completes the sentence grammatically and contextually:

The committee decided to ______ the proposal until further data could be collected.
questions.solutionText: Solution "The committee decided to ______ the proposal until further data could be collected." (1) Defer: to put off to a later time; delay. Used with decisions or actions, and commonly followed by "until". Fits grammatically and contextually. (2) Suspend: to temporarily stop or halt something already in progress. Does not naturally pair with "until further data could be collected". (3) Postpone: to delay to a later time. Very similar to defer, but "postpone" is typically used for events or meetings, while "defer" is more naturally used with decisions or proposals. (4) Cancel: to call off entirely. Contradicts "until further data could be collected", which implies a temporary delay, not permanent cancellation.
questions.solution: Solution "The committee decided to ______ the proposal until further data could be collected." (1) Defer: to put off to a later time; delay. Used with decisions or actions, and commonly followed by "until". Fits grammatically and contextually. (2) Suspend: to temporarily stop or halt something already in progress. Does not naturally pair with "until further data could be collected". (3) Postpone: to delay to a later time. Very similar to defer, but "postpone" is typically used for events or meetings, while "defer" is more naturally used with decisions or proposals. (4) Cancel: to call off entirely. Contradicts "until further data could be collected", which implies a temporary delay, not permanent cancellation.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
"The committee decided to ______ the proposal until further data could be collected."

(1) Defer: to put off to a later time; delay. Used with decisions or actions, and commonly followed by "until". Fits grammatically and contextually.

(2) Suspend: to temporarily stop or halt something already in progress. Does not naturally pair with "until further data could be collected".

(3) Postpone: to delay to a later time. Very similar to defer, but "postpone" is typically used for events or meetings, while "defer" is more naturally used with decisions or proposals.

(4) Cancel: to call off entirely. Contradicts "until further data could be collected", which implies a temporary delay, not permanent cancellation.

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Paragraph: Before the invention of the mechanical clock in the late 13th century, time was a fluid concept governed by the natural rhythms of the sun and the changing of the seasons. Sundials and water clocks provided rough estimates of the hour, but their accuracy was highly dependent on weather conditions and the time of year. The mechanical clock, initially developed by European monks to strictly regulate the schedule of daily prayers, introduced a radical shift. Time was no longer a continuous, natural flow; it became a sequence of quantifiable, discrete units. This mechanization of time gradually spread from monasteries to town squares. As public clocks became the focal point of civic life, merchants and artisans began to synchronize their labor to the chiming of the hour, rather than the rising of the sun. Consequently, the mechanical clock did more than merely measure time; it transformed it from an abstract natural phenomenon into a manageable commodity, fundamentally altering human productivity and societal organization. Question: Which of the following best states the primary purpose of the passage?

RETICENT: inclined to be silent; not willing to speak freely or reveal one's thoughts.

(1) Diffident: shy and lacking self-confidence. (Similar to reticent, not opposite)

(2) Taciturn: habitually silent or uncommunicative. (Synonym of reticent, not opposite)

(3) Circumspect: cautious and careful in one's actions or words. (Similar restraint as reticent, not opposite)

(4) Forthcoming: willing to share information openly; ready to communicate. (Directly opposite of reticent)

questions.questionHtml:
In the question given below, a sentence with two blanks is given. Choose the option that best completes the sentence grammatically and contextually.

Despite the larger-than-life portrayal of the protagonist in the film Dhurandhar, critics noted that the narrative occasionally ______ into melodrama, thereby ______ the otherwise gritty realism of the story.
questions.solutionText: Solution (1) escalates; reinforcing → escalating into melodrama is awkward, and reinforcing realism contradicts the negative tone set by "despite" (2) diverges; preserving → diverging into melodrama is unnatural, and preserving realism contradicts the criticism (3) culminates; amplifying → culminates implies a final peak, not an occasional slip, and amplifying realism contradicts the context (4) lapses; diluting → "lapses into melodrama" is a natural collocation meaning occasionally falling into melodrama, and "diluting the gritty realism" logically follows as a negative consequence
questions.solution: Solution (1) escalates; reinforcing → escalating into melodrama is awkward, and reinforcing realism contradicts the negative tone set by "despite" (2) diverges; preserving → diverging into melodrama is unnatural, and preserving realism contradicts the criticism (3) culminates; amplifying → culminates implies a final peak, not an occasional slip, and amplifying realism contradicts the context (4) lapses; diluting → "lapses into melodrama" is a natural collocation meaning occasionally falling into melodrama, and "diluting the gritty realism" logically follows as a negative consequence
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(1) escalates; reinforcing → escalating into melodrama is awkward, and reinforcing realism contradicts the negative tone set by "despite"

(2) diverges; preserving → diverging into melodrama is unnatural, and preserving realism contradicts the criticism

(3) culminates; amplifying → culminates implies a final peak, not an occasional slip, and amplifying realism contradicts the context

(4) lapses; diluting → "lapses into melodrama" is a natural collocation meaning occasionally falling into melodrama, and "diluting the gritty realism" logically follows as a negative consequence

questions.html: Amongst the following figures, find the correct one, if it is known that the total number of dots on opposite faces of cube shown is always 7.
questions.questionHtml: Amongst the following figures, find the correct one, if it is known that the total number of dots on opposite faces of cube shown is always 7.
questions.solutionText: Solution Since the total number of dots on opposite faces is always 7, therefore 1 dot must lie opposite 6 dots, 2 dots must lie opposite 5 dots and 3 dots must lie opposite 4 dots. In the figures (2) and (4), 2 dots appear adjacent to 5 dots, and in fig. (3), 3 dots appear adjacent to 4 dots. Hence, figures are incorrect. Therefore, only fig. (1) is correct.
